Off Calendar Feb-14-2024 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Atty. EVAN BARR, Esq. of Law Offices of Felicia C. Curran (Tel: 510-823-2331) for Plaintiffs (via Zoom videoconference).
Atty. VLADIMIR ANDRICH, Esq. and JASON CURLIANO, Esq. of Buty & Curliano LLP (Tel: 510-267-3000) for Defendants.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT KAISER FOUNDATION HEALTH PLAN, INC., KAISER FOUNDATION HOSPITALS, THE PERMANENTE MEDICAL GROUP, INC.'s DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

The Kaiser defendants' demurrer to the first amended complaint (FAC) is overruled.
The FAC adequately pleads negligence and wrongful death against Kaiser Foundation Health Plan, Inc. (Id. at 19:28-21:2, 26:21-27:2.) The demurrer is based on the notion that the FAC "pleads a claim under Civil Code 3428," but it does not. (Memo. 4:9: Opp. 4:24-25.) Moreover, defendants and others may be jointly and severally liable, and agency theories are pled. (PacifiCare of California v. Bright Medical Associates, Inc. (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 1451, 1463; FAC 6:19-22.)
The FAC adequately pleads elder abuse/neglect against the Kaiser defendants. (Id. at 21:3-25:8; CACI 3103.) The Kaiser defendants' control over decedent's healthcare and his hospitalization suffice to allege a "substantial caretaking or custodial relationship" at the pleading stage. (Id.; see also Stewart v. Sup. Ct. (2017) 16 Cal.App.5th 87.)

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RBU)

Notice Of Defendant Hebrew Home For Aged Disabled Dba The Lynn & Roy M. Frank Residences And As The San Francisco Campus For Jewish Living'S Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Action
Off Calendar Jul-27-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.

RE: Defendant HEBREW HOME FOR AGED DISABLED Dba The Lynn & Roy M. Frank Residences And As The San Francisco Campus For Jewish Living's Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Action.

The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant's motion to compel arbitration is denied. Defendant seeks to individually bind decedent's daughter, Maria Ayerdi, to arbitration provisions in a document she signed as her father's personal representative upon his admission to its aged-disabled facility. (MPA, 5:13-16.) Our case is close kin to Lopez v. Bartlett Care Center, LLC (2019) 39 Cal.App.5th 311. It held that language in an arbitration agreement was procedurally unconscionable. (Id. at 313.) The Lopez agreement on its face was between the resident and the facility, it did not warn that a resident's representative was waiving individual trial rights and the signature block failed to warn of the purported dual capacity in which the representative was signing. Such is also so here. The document in our case is titled "Residence and Care Agreement," implying a contract between decedent (the resident) and the facility for his care. (See Sarison Decl., Ex. 2.) No language warns that the individual sacrifices her trial rights when signing as a resident's personal representative. (Id.) Indeed, the key sentence is buried in a block of text and not bolded like other key points on the page. (Id.) Further, the document's signature block fails to warn of the purported dual capacity in which a representative is signing. (Id.) Our arbitration clause is also substantively unconscionable. It lacks mutuality, requiring arbitration of claims residents are most likely to bring against the facility (e.g., personal injury or wrongful death), while allowing the facility to pursue in court legal actions the facility is most likely to bring against residents (evictions or small claims court actions). The Lopez agreement contained a similar provision, which rendered it substantively unconscionable. (39 Cal.App.5th at 321-22.) Thus, Maria's claims are not subject to arbitration, as the agreement is unconscionable. The same is true of the claims by Maria's five non-signatory sibling-plaintiffs. A court may also deny arbitration where "[a] party to an arbitration agreement is also a party to a pending court action...with a third party arising out of the same transaction or series of related transactions and there is a possibility of conflicting rulings on a common issue of law or fact." (CCP 1281.2(c).) Maria's siblings qualify as such third parties.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not reported. (302/RBU)
